footer.description: An open, web-based approach to publishing and sharing cultural collections.

untranslatedNotice.title: Translation in progress
untranslatedNotice.body: This page is not translated into Dutch yet, so you are seeing the English content with the shared Dutch site shell.

hero.headlineHtml: Webcollections are like websites for cultural collections.
hero.leadHtml:
The Open Collections Protocol makes it simple to publish and share cultural collections on the web in an open format built on plain web standards. No database required.

Host them anywhere, link them across the web, and keep them reusable over time.

intro.title: Collections that behave like websites
intro.body: Open Collections treats collections as web resources. You can host them on your own infrastructure, publish them with your existing workflows, and keep them readable over time.
features: Host collections anywhere on the web
Link collections across sites and systems
Reuse collections in tools and platforms
Keep a portable format based on plain web standards

pathways.title: Start from the files, records, or datasets you already have
paragraphs: Many collections already begin as local folders, public media URLs, exported records from spreadsheets, or broader datasets harvested or imported from existing systems. Open Collections helps you work with those starting points without forcing everything into one complicated workflow from the start.

Not every source body is itself a collection. You can begin with files on disk, records from current systems, or a larger dataset, then select the records, files, and links that belong in a more focused public-facing collection.

curation.title: From datasets to curated collections
paragraphs: Open Collections helps you work with datasets and turn selected records, files, and links into curated collections. Datasets are broad source bodies; collections are curated selections.

Collections are intentional. Open Collections helps curators and collection teams shape focused collections for browsing, sharing, teaching, research, or storytelling while continuing to add value on top of existing systems and datasets.

Collections can also build on existing records and media over time. The same item may appear in more than one collection, with each collection adding its own context, links, and interpretation. Open Collections supports this broader idea of open and linked collections while keeping each collection clear and purposeful.

audience.title: Designed for archives, museums, and design libraries
audience.body: Whether you are working with archival material, object photography, exhibition assets, material libraries, or product collections, Open Collections helps bring files and metadata together in a way that stays understandable for teams and visitors.

workflow.title: Curate collections for real use
workflow.body: Use the Collection Manager to select, shape, and structure a collection. Use the Collection Browser to explore it in a clear visual interface. This helps teams carry out curation work internally and makes it easier to share focused collections online.

steps.title: Gather
steps.body: Bring together local files, existing media links, or exported records from your current collection work.

steps.title: Curate
steps.body: Use Collection Manager to select and structure items, media references, and details so teams can shape collections with confidence.

steps.title: Browse and share
steps.body: Open the result in Collection Browser so colleagues and audiences can explore and use the collection.

registry.title: Explore registered collections
registry.body: Browse recently registered collections to see how organizations are publishing collections on the web.
registry.widgetIntro: A quick view of recent registrations from the Open Collections registry.

cards.title: Get started
cards.body: Create your first collection from files, links, or exported records.

cards.title: Explore the tools
cards.body: See the Collection Manager and Collection Browser.

cards.title: Host your collection
cards.body: Learn where and how to host your collection on the web.

cards.title: Read the docs
cards.body: Learn about hosting, protocol details, and implementation.

hero.title: Get started with the materials you already have
hero.body: Use this page for a calm, practical first run. You can begin with local files, existing media links, or exported records and move toward a shareable collection in a few steps.

sections.title: 1. Choose a starting point
list: Gather the files, folders, links, or exported records that belong in one collection.
Use clear titles or file names and remove anything you do not want to share.
Keep enough context together that browsing will feel understandable later.

sections.title: 2. Open Collection Manager
paragraphs: Open Collection Manager in your browser and start a new collection for the material you want to organize.

sections.title: 3. Add items from files, links, or records
paragraphs: Some collections start from a folder on disk. Others begin with metadata from a spreadsheet or with items that point to media already hosted on another site. Use the path that matches what you already have.

sections.title: 4. Add titles and details
paragraphs: Add a collection title, short description, and item details that help people understand what they are viewing, whether the media is uploaded locally or referenced from an existing URL.

sections.title: 5. Simple example: publish metadata with referenced media
paragraphs: Create a collection, add two items, and use existing public image URLs for the media. Then add titles and short descriptions so the collection can be published and shared while the media stays on its current host.

sections.title: 6. Save and share
paragraphs: Save your collection, then share the URL with your team or audience. You can keep improving the records, media links, and presentation over time.

hero.title: Docs
hero.body: Guidance for both end users and implementation teams. Use these pages for practical setup help, architecture, integration, and reference details.
